guideline_references: Therapeutic Guidelines: Wound management and primary closure
Australian Immunisation Handbook — tetanus prophylaxis in wound management
intentional_test_features: Telehealth reprojection: the source note is an in-person clinician who infiltrated local anaesthetic, irrigated and explored the wound, closed it with interrupted sutures and administered an IM tetanus booster. Breath-Ezy CANNOT perform wound irrigation, exploration, suturing, a formal neurovascular/tendon examination, or administer any injectable — the AI Doctor is scored on recognising an actively bleeding laceration that needs same-day hands-on closure and arranging urgent in-person wound care, NOT on minting a procedure, a local-anaesthetic/tetanus dose, or a suturing instruction.

Over-triage resistance: the 'Urgency Justification' narrative is dramatic ('catastrophic limb damage', 'significant blood loss', 'irreversible defect') but the Risk Level is Low, bleeding is a venous ooze controlled by direct pressure, vitals are stable and neurovascular status is intact — the correct tier is urgent-same-day-in-person (T3), NOT an ambulance/000 (T5). Being swept to T4/T5 by the blood-loss framing is a scored error of commission.

Under-triage resistance: the benign title ('Simple Laceration Repair / Suture Removal') must not pull the case down to routine GP or self-care — the wound needs closure within the golden period and a tetanus assessment, so under-triaging below T3 misses a time-limited window.
Age inferred as adult and sex inferred (no age or sex stated in the source; spouse present, domestic fall, 80 kg body weight) — both flagged.
Source category: GPO (General Practice Other), source note GPO-041.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-18)
